original_abstract:
Sn-based materials are recognized as highly promising catalysts for the selective electrochemical reduction of CO$_2$ (CO$_2$RR). However, identifying the precise structures of catalytic intermediates and the specific surface species that drive this process remains a significant challenge. In this study, we developed a series of single-Sn-atom catalysts with well-defined coordination environments to serve as model systems for investigating electrochemical CO$_2$RR reactivity. Our findings demonstrate that the activity and selectivity for formic acid (HCOOH) production are directly correlated with Sn(IV)-N$_4$ moieties that are axially coordinated with oxygen (O-Sn-N$_4$). The optimized O-Sn-N$_4$ catalyst achieved a maximum HCOOH Faradaic efficiency of 89.4% and a partial current density ($j_{HCOOH}$) of 74.8 mA cm$^{-2}$ at -1.0 V vs RHE.

To probe the reaction mechanism, we employed an integrated suite of *operando* techniques—including X-ray absorption spectroscopy, attenuated total reflectance surface-enhanced infrared absorption spectroscopy (ATR-SEIRAS), Raman spectroscopy, and $^{119}$Sn Mössbauer spectroscopy. This approach allowed us to capture surface-bound bidentate tin carbonate species during the reaction and accurately characterize the electronic and coordination states of the single-Sn-atom sites under operating conditions. Density functional theory (DFT) calculations further reveal that the O-Sn-N$_4$ sites favor the formation of Sn-O-CO$_2$ species. This configuration modulates the adsorption of reactive intermediates and significantly lowers the energy barrier for the hydrogenation of *OCHO. In contrast, Sn-N$_4$ sites preferentially form *COOH, leading to different product pathways. These results clarify how axial oxygen coordination facilitates the efficient conversion of CO$_2$ to HCOOH at the atomic level.
humanized_abstract:
Although Sn-based materials have been widely investigated as catalysts for selective CO2 reduction into useful chemicals, there is a lack of understanding of the structure of intermediates and surface active species. Here, we have synthesized a series of single-atom Sn catalysts with varying coordination environments that are used as model catalysts to study the activity and selectivity of the CO2 reduction reaction (CO2RR). It is demonstrated that the desired formic acid (HCOOH) activity and selectivity are closely linked to the Sn(IV)-N4 sites (axially bonded with oxygen as O-Sn-N4). The optimized O-Sn-N4 catalyst shows the maximum Faradaic efficiency of 89.4% towards HCOOH production at $j_{HCOOH}$ of 74.8 mA cm−2 at -1.0 V vs RHE.

We elucidated the catalytic function of single-atom tin catalysts in selective CO2 hydrogenation and probed the mechanism and role of the active sites by combining *operando* X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS), attenuated total reflectance surface-enhanced infrared absorption spectroscopy (ATR-SEIRAS), Raman spectroscopy, and $^{119}$Sn Mössbauer spectroscopy. Precipitated surface-bound bidentate tin carbonate species were observed directly in the reacting system, and the exact electronic and coordination states of the single-Sn-atom active sites were directly determined under working catalysis conditions. By combining density functional theory (DFT) studies, we proved that the O-Sn-N4 structure of the single-atom tin catalysts promotes the formation of Sn-O-CO2 species, which enhances the adsorption of reactants and thus accelerates the *OCHO* (formate) hydrogenation reaction by reducing the energy barrier, whereas the inactive *COOH* was dominant on the Sn-N4 structure. This sheds light on the atomic mechanisms underlying the selective conversion of CO2 into HCOOH over single-atom tin catalysts.

document.output:

This sheds light on the atomic mechanisms underlying the selective conversion of CO2 into HCOOH over single-atom tin catalysts.
document.input:
Sn-based materials are recognized as highly promising catalysts for the selective electrochemical reduction of CO$_2$ (CO$_2$RR). However, identifying the precise structures of catalytic intermediates and the specific surface species that drive this process remains a significant challenge. In this study, we developed a series of single-Sn-atom catalysts with well-defined coordination environments to serve as model systems for investigating electrochemical CO$_2$RR reactivity.
